comments.text: I dont understand the part "furthermore with squarefree terms", because by simply taking $a+a$ for some term of $A$, we immediately have that $a$ must be squarefree.

The paper Terence Tao and I just uploaded is mostly aimed at resolving #1102, but we have some results for this problem as well. In particular, we improve upon the $0$ density result in my earlier comment and, in the other direction, we show the existence of a sequence with sub-exponential growth. More precisely, define $\mathcal{SF} \subset \mathbb{N}$ to be the set of squarefree integers, let $kA$ be the set of $k$ (not necessarily distinct) elements of $A$, and let us say that $A$ has squarefree sums if $2A \subset \mathcal{SF}$. We then have the following results: Theorem 1. For any sequence $A = \{a_1 < a_2 < \cdots \} \subset \mathbb{N}$ with squarefree sums, we have $a_j > 0.24j^{4/3}$ for all $j \in \mathbb{N}$. Theorem 2. There exists a sequence $A = \{a_1 < a_2 < \cdots \} \subset \mathcal{SF}$ with squarefree sums for which $a_j < \exp(5j / \log j)$ holds for all large enough $j \in \mathbb{N}$. We furthermore observe that the proofs of Theorem 1 and 2 can be modified to obtain the following two bonus results on $k$-free sums and sums of three elements respectively: Theorem 3. For any $k \ge 2$ and any sequence $A = \{a_1 < a_2 < \cdots \} \subset \mathbb{N}$ with $k$-free sums, we have $a_j \gg j^{2k/(2k-1)}$ for all $j \in \mathbb{N}$. Theorem 4. There exists an absolute constant $C$ and a sequence $A = \{a_1 < a_2 < \cdots \}$ with $A \cup 2A \cup 3A \subset \mathcal{SF}$ for which $a_j < \exp(Cj^2 / \log j)$ holds for all $j \in \mathbb{N}$. Note that Theorem 4 can not be extended to sums of four elements, as $4A$ of course contains elements divisible by $4$, if $A$ is non-empty. EDIT: Some of our results have turned out to be obsolete by the results from Konyagin mentioned below, so Terry and I rewrote the relevant parts of our paper. The original arXiv version containing proofs of the above theorems can be found here. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Benjamin Bedert has pointed out to us that there is in fact a significant body of literature on this problem already, namely * P. Erdős and A. Sárközy. On divisibility properties of integers of the form a + a′. Acta Math. Hungar., 50(1- 2):117–122, 1987. doi:10.1007/BF01903370. * S. V. Konyagin. Problems of the set of square-free numbers. Izv. Ross. Akad. Nauk Ser. Mat., 68(3):63–90, 2004. doi:10.1070/IM2004v068n03ABEH000486. * G. N. Sárközy. On a problem of P. Erdős. Acta Math. Hungar., 60(3-4):271–282, 1992. doi:10.1007/BF00051645. * Katalin Gyarmati. On divisibility properties of integers of the form ab + 1. Period. Math. Hungar., 43(1-2):71–79, 2001. doi:10.1023/A:1015229531017. In particular, the results of Konyagin give the improved lower bound $$ a_j \gg j^{15/11} \exp\left( -O\left(\frac{\log j}{\sqrt{\log\log j}} \right)\right).$$ Konyagin also constructs subsets of $\{1,\dots,N\}$ with squarefree sums of cardinality $\gg \log^2 N \log\log N$. If there was a way to paste these constructions together for various $N$, this would give an example with $$ a_j \ll \exp( O( j^{1/2} / \log^{1/2} j ))$$ for all $j$, but we have not checked whether such a pasting is possible. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Thanks! I see that Erdős and Sárközy were just looking at the finite case, which (although obviously closely related) feels like a different enough problem to me that I've separated it at [1109]. Hopefully I've managed to capture all the relevant results and references between the two problems, please let me know if not. (I believe there is a typo in your Konyagin lower bound, the exponent should be $15/11$.)

For a prime $p$, let $A_p$ be the set of residue classes $x \pmod{p^2}$ for which at least two distinct $a \in A$ exist with $a \equiv x \pmod{p^2}$. Then $|A_p| < \frac{p^2}{2}$ for all $p$ by the assumption that $A + A$ only contains squarefree integers. Taking the product $M = (p_1p_2 \cdots p_k)^2$ of distinct primes $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_k$ then gives that the number of residue classes $x \pmod{M}$ that occur more than once among the $a \in A$ is smaller than $\frac{M}{2^k}$. By the infinitude of primes we therefore obtain $A \cap \{1, 2, \ldots, x\} = o(x)$. I understand that this is a terrible bound which is clearly improvable, but hey, you have to start somewhere!
